solution: Since \( -1 \le \sin(\tfrac{[[b]]}{x}) \le 1 \), we have \( -x^{2} \le x^{2}\sin(\tfrac{[[b]]}{x}) \le x^{2} \). Both bounds \( \to 0 \), so by the Squeeze Theorem the oscillating term \( \to 0 \) and the limit is \( [[a]] = [[answer]] \).

solution: Since \( -1 \le \sin([[c]]x) \le 1 \), the fraction is squeezed: \( -\dfrac{[[b]]}{x} \le \dfrac{[[b]]\sin([[c]]x)}{x} \le \dfrac{[[b]]}{x} \) (for \( [[b]]>0 \)), and both bounds \( \to 0 \) as \( x\to\infty \). So the oscillating fraction vanishes, leaving \( [[a]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds \( b \), but the bounded fraction contributes 0 as \( x\to\infty \).
This subtracts \( b \), but the bounded fraction contributes 0.
This keeps only the numerator's coefficient and drops the constant \( a \).

stem: Suppose a function \( g \) satisfies \( [[a]] - [[b]](x - ([[p]]))^{2} \le g(x) \le [[a]] + [[b]](x - ([[p]]))^{2} \) for all real \( x \). Using the Squeeze Theorem, evaluate \( \lim_{x \to [[p]]} g(x) \).
solution: Both the lower bound \( [[a]] - [[b]](x-[[p]])^{2} \) and the upper bound \( [[a]] + [[b]](x-[[p]])^{2} \) tend to \( [[a]] \) as \( x \to [[p]] \). Since \( g \) is trapped between them, the Squeeze Theorem forces \( \lim_{x \to [[p]]} g(x) = [[a]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the coefficient \( b \), but both bounding functions converge to \( a \) alone.
This subtracts the coefficient \( b \), but both bounding functions converge to \( a \) alone.
This reports only the coefficient of the squared term, dropping \( a \).
This assumes the squeezed value is always 0.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Suppose a function \( g \) satisfies \( \lvert g(x) - [[a]] \rvert \le [[b]]\lvert x - ([[p]]) \rvert \) for all real \( x \) near \( [[p]] \). Using the Squeeze Theorem, evaluate \( \lim_{x \to [[p]]} g(x) \).
solution: The inequality \( -[[b]]|x-[[p]]| \le g(x) - [[a]] \le [[b]]|x-[[p]]| \) squeezes \( g(x) - [[a]] \) between two functions that both \( \to 0 \) as \( x \to [[p]] \). Hence \( g(x) - [[a]] \to 0 \), so \( \lim_{x \to [[p]]} g(x) = [[a]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds \( b \), but the bound on the deviation forces it to vanish entirely.
This subtracts \( b \), but the bound on the deviation forces it to vanish entirely.
This reports only the bounding coefficient, dropping \( a \).
This assumes the limit is always 0 regardless of \( a \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.
